nativeRange.habitat: Found on reef slopes in clear to moderately turbid water at shallow to mid-depths. Large colonies are commonly found in shaded positions such as under reef overhangs.

diet.notes: Primarily photosynthetic via zooxanthellae. Supplemental target-feeding of small meaty foods such as Mysis shrimp, brine shrimp, or coral-specific foods weekly is generally recommended to support growth and polyp extension, though the coral can survive on light alone in well-established reef systems.

avoidWith: aggressive LPS corals with long sweeper tentacles

imageCandidates.notes: MDC Seamarc Maldives; 3648x2736. In-situ whole colony, sharp, vibrant green with the characteristic small evenly-spaced corallites and submassive encrusting form.
MDC Seamarc Maldives; 3648x2736. Tack-sharp macro clearly showing the diagnostic green, evenly-spaced corallites; tight crop so kept as a detail extra rather than the primary pick.
John Turnbull; 3103x2225. In-situ large flat encrusting colony showing the massive growth form; correctly ID'd but paler temperate-form coloration, so non-recommended.

summary: The Plesiastrea Coral (Plesiastrea versipora), also sold as Lesser Star Coral or Moon Brain Coral, is a large polyp stony coral from the Indo-Pacific generally considered beginner-friendly. It grows in an encrusting form, tolerates a range of lighting conditions, and is often confused with Cyphastrea in the trade.
careNotes:
The Plesiastrea Coral is the only species of its genus commonly encountered in the aquarium hobby. The genus Plesiastrea was long considered monotypic — containing just P. versipora — though a second species, P. peroni, was resurrected in 2022; it favors cooler subtropical habitats and does not appear in the trade. It is frequently confused with Cyphastrea species in the aquarium hobby because both share similarly small, evenly spaced corallites and an encrusting growth form; Plesiastrea tends to have slightly more fleshy polyps and somewhat greater spacing between corallites. The genus was formerly placed in the family Faviidae alongside many other brain and star corals, but is now recognized in its own distinct family.

Growth form: The Plesiastrea Coral grows in an encrusting to submassive form, spreading across rock and substrate rather than building dramatically upward. Wild colonies can become quite large — documented specimens have reached up to 1 m in thickness in high-latitude habitats. In captivity, colonies generally stay within the 10–40 cm range.

Lighting: Moderate light intensity is generally recommended, roughly 75–200 PAR. New specimens are best introduced at the lower end of that range and acclimated gradually. Unlike more demanding SPS corals (small-polyp stony corals such as Acropora), the Plesiastrea Coral tends to be tolerant of a fairly wide range of light levels and can do well in shaded placements or under overhangs.

Flow: Moderate, indirect water movement is generally preferred. Direct, laminar flow aimed at the coral should be avoided, as it can stress the tissue.

Placement: This coral is flexible in placement — it will encrust onto whatever surface it contacts and can spread laterally from most positions in the aquarium.

Aggression: The Plesiastrea Coral is generally considered low-aggression and does not appear to extend the long sweeper tentacles that more aggressive LPS corals like Favites or Goniastrea are known to deploy. As a standard precaution, maintaining 2–3 cm of spacing from neighboring corals is advisable.

Feeding: Like most reef corals, Plesiastrea versipora is zooxanthellate — it hosts photosynthetic algae within its tissue that provide the bulk of its energy through light. Weekly supplemental target-feeding with small meaty foods such as Mysis shrimp, Cyclops nauplii, or coral amino acid preparations has been reported to support coloration and growth. The species is also documented to extend its tentacles during the day in the wild, suggesting it is capable of capturing small suspended food particles.

Water chemistry: Standard reef water parameters apply — alkalinity 8–9 dKH, calcium 400–450 ppm, magnesium 1250–1400 ppm, salinity SG 1.025–1.026, temperature 24–28°C, pH 8.0–8.4. The Plesiastrea Coral is generally considered more tolerant of modest parameter fluctuations than demanding SPS species, which contributes to its reputation as a good choice for beginner reef aquariums.

Aquacultured specimens turn up periodically at specialty coral retailers, with named color morphs such as 'Neon Plesiastrea' and 'Mint Chip Plesiastrea' — both trending toward bright green coloration — appearing from time to time. Stock is not always consistently available, so sourcing may require some patience. Wild-collected specimens may occasionally appear in the trade as well.

Note: P. versipora is one of the more eurythermal reef corals known — it thrives across both tropical reefs and higher-latitude subtropical and temperate marine environments in the wild. Aquarium care should target standard tropical reef parameters.
breedingNotes: Propagation in captivity is done by fragging — cutting a small section of the colony with bone cutters or a bandsaw and attaching it to a frag plug or piece of rubble, where it encrusts readily. In the wild, Plesiastrea versipora reproduces sexually through broadcast spawning. Captive sexual breeding is not practiced in the aquarium hobby.

primary.name: World Wide Corals – Plesiastrea product listings
primary.notes: Product listings for WWC Mint Chip Plesiastrea and WWC Neon Plesiastrea confirmed care level Easy, lighting Medium, flow Medium. Full numeric water parameters not provided on product pages.

additional.name: WoRMS – Plesiastrea versipora (AphiaID 207494)
additional.notes: Used for taxonomy (Family Plesiastreidae, Order Scleractinia), native range (Indo-Pacific, 64 documented distribution records), and habitat description. Confirmed: reef slopes shallow to mid-depths, shaded overhangs; colonies can reach 3 m in high-latitude habitats; zooxanthellate; daytime tentacle extension noted.

additional.name: Wikipedia – Plesiastrea versipora
additional.notes: Used for growth form confirmation (encrusting/massive, up to 1 m thickness), long-lived colony documentation, and range (Indian and Pacific Oceans). Not used for husbandry parameters.

additional.name: ReefBuilders – Plesiastrea identification and taxonomy
additional.notes: Confirmed flat-to-semi-lobed growth form, small corallites, frequent confusion with Cyphastrea, aquacultured availability, Indo-Pacific distribution. Husbandry parameters not covered.

additional.name: ReefChasers – Cyphastrea Coral Care Guide (cross-reference for closely related faviid LPS)
additional.notes: Used as secondary cross-reference for water parameters of a closely related faviid LPS coral (Cyphastrea, formerly in same family Faviidae). Confirmed: temperature 77–84°F (25–29°C), pH 8.0–8.4, alkalinity 8–9 dKH, calcium 400–450 ppm, lighting 100–200 PAR, flow moderate, care beginner. Values consistent with Favites (another faviid) guide from same source.
